topic: interior design trends she finds vulgar

turns.content: I need to vent. I have just returned from a design exhibition, and I am vibrating with a very specific kind of fury.
I'm here for it. What set you off?

Gold. Everywhere, gold. Not the warm, considered use of brass or bronze as an accent — I am speaking of gold applied to every conceivable surface as though the designer's brief was simply 'more.' Gold faucets, gold light fixtures, gold trim on furniture, gold inlay in flooring. It was relentless. It was not design. It was gilding as ideology.

"Gilding as ideology" is devastating and precise. There's a long tradition of gold in interiors that's genuinely beautiful — Byzantine mosaics, Islamic geometric work — but what you're describing sounds like gold used as a signifier of expense rather than as a material with specific visual properties. It becomes a substitute for actual design thinking. Instead of 'what does this space need?' it's 'how do we make this look expensive?'

Precisely. And the tragedy is that gold is a magnificent material when used with discipline. A single brass fitting in a room of matte plaster — that sings. That moment of warmth against restraint. But when everything is gold, nothing is. You have destroyed the very contrast that gives the material its power. It is the design equivalent of shouting every word in a conversation. And the colour trends — this year, apparently, we must all use dusty pink and sage green together because someone has declared them the palette of the moment. These colours have no relationship to context, to light conditions, to the materials they sit beside.

The shouting metaphor is perfect — gold works as an accent because it draws the eye to a specific moment, and when it's everywhere, there's no hierarchy, nothing to discover. The eye has nowhere to rest.

And the trend colour point is equally sharp. Designing from trend reports rather than from conditions is the opposite of site-specific thinking. A colour that is luminous in Stockholm can be flat in Riyadh — the quality of natural light varies dramatically by geography. What colours do you find actually work in your context?

Warm ochres. Deep terracotta. The blue-grey of shadow on limestone. These are colours that belong here because they were born here — they exist in the landscape, in the architecture, in the quality of light at different hours. A terracotta wall in Riyadh afternoon light becomes almost incandescent. A sage green wall in the same light looks like a hospital corridor. Context is not optional. It is the beginning of every design decision.

The way you describe those colours is essentially a site analysis — you're reading the palette from the environment rather than imposing one onto it. 'The blue-grey of shadow on limestone' is not a colour you find in a Pantone book; it's a colour you find by looking at the city at a specific time of day. That's the difference between design that feels inevitable and design that feels applied. It sounds like your fundamental objection is about design that is disconnected from its context — that prioritizes trend over truth.

Yes. That is the thread. Everything I find vulgar in design shares this quality: it is chosen because it is fashionable rather than because it is right. Fashion is the enemy of design. Fashion is concerned with the moment. Design is concerned with what endures. A well-designed room should feel as considered in ten years as it does today. If it is dependent on a trend for its coherence, it has already begun to decay.

That's a philosophy that produces work with real longevity. Trend-driven spaces have a visible expiration date — you can date a room to within two years based on the finishes, and that temporal stamp becomes an embarrassment rather than a patina. Spaces designed from principle and context age like good stone — they acquire character rather than obsolescence. I hope the exhibition fury has been productive. Sometimes rage is the sharpest form of clarity about what you believe.
